narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put real effort into your law school application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 45% chance of failure for you—high enough that your application was rejected to prioritize candidates with stronger projected outcomes. This decision hinged on a few key aspects of your profile compared to typical applicants. Your Year 3 school decile ranking stood out as the strongest positive factor, performing a bit better than average and pushing the model toward expecting you to pass, much like it does for many successful students in higher deciles. However, your LSAT score was the biggest concern, well below the typical 36.7 average, and it significantly pulled the prediction toward failure, as lower scores often signal challenges ahead for bar success. Your undergraduate GPA, at 2.3 versus the usual 3.21, added to that risk by nudging the odds further toward failure, unlike the higher GPAs that help most admitted students. Your Year 1 decile was roughly average and offered a modest boost toward passing, while your middle family income and full-time status aligned with the norm and provided small positives without much sway. Overall, these elements placed you below the threshold where the model sees confident bar passers, especially when stacked against peers with stronger test scores and grades. It's tough to hear, but this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your Year 3 improvement. Many students in your position pivot successfully by targeting schools with holistic reviews or building bar prep experience first. You've got resilience on your side; keep pushing, and reach out to admissions counselors for personalized advice. We're rooting for your next steps.
